report:
## Profitability

PepsiCo and Coca-Cola are both highly profitable beverage-and-snack giants, but their profitability profiles differ meaningfully in scale, margin structure, and recent trajectory. PEP is the larger business by revenue—$91.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q2] versus KO's $47.1B [KO:Q2]—yet KO consistently converts a higher share of revenue into profit. KO's gross margin runs well above PEP's: in FY2025, KO generated $29.5B of gross profit on $47.9B of revenue [KO:Q15][KO:Q3], implying ~62% gross margin, while PEP produced $50.9B of gross profit on $93.9B of revenue [PEP:Q15][PEP:Q3], implying ~54% gross margin—a gap that reflects PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and snack manufacturing.

At the operating income level, KO reported $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11], implying an operating margin of roughly 29% on its ~$47.9B revenue base. However, this figure warrants context: KO's FY2024 operating income was approximately $9.99B (~21% margin), meaning the FY2025 result reflects a sharp ~38% single-year surge that may include non-recurring items and should not necessarily be taken as representative of KO's normalized operating profitability. By comparison, PEP's operating income fell from $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10] to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11]—a ~10.8% decline—on a revenue base nearly double KO's, implying an operating margin of roughly 12%. This deterioration extends well beyond the bottom line and is not solely attributable to below-the-line items, indicating a broader profitability headwind for PEP.

Net income tells a similar story: KO earned $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7] versus PEP's $8.2B [PEP:Q7], a notable reversal given PEP's much larger top line. KO's net income surged from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6] to $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7], though this followed a slight year-over-year dip from FY2023's $10.7B, and the FY2025 jump may reflect non-recurring items given the ~38% rise in operating income over the same period. PEP's net income, meanwhile, declined from its FY2024 peak of $9.6B [PEP:Q6] to $8.2B in FY2025 [PEP:Q7], widening the gap in KO's favor. Both companies have grown revenues over the FY2022–FY2025 period, though PEP's growth was essentially flat between FY2023 and FY2024 (~0.4%), making "steady growth" an overstatement for that interval.

**PEP:** PepsiCo is a revenue powerhouse, growing from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], but its diversified food-and-beverage model constrains margins relative to KO, with gross margins in the mid-50% range and operating margins around 12–14%. Both operating income and net income deteriorated in FY2025—operating income fell ~10.8% to $11.5B [PEP:Q11] and net income declined to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] from a FY2024 peak of $9.6B [PEP:Q6]—signaling meaningful near-term profitability pressure that extends across the income statement even as the top line continued to grow modestly.

**KO:** Coca-Cola's asset-light, concentrate-focused model delivers structurally superior margins: gross profit reached $29.5B on $47.9B of revenue in FY2025 [KO:Q15][KO:Q3], implying ~62% gross margin. Operating income of $13.8B [KO:Q11] implies an operating margin near 29% for FY2025, though this represents a sharp jump from ~21% in FY2024 and may be elevated by non-recurring items, so the normalized margin likely sits somewhat below that level. Net income surged to $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7] from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6]—itself a slight dip from FY2023's $10.7B—demonstrating strong recent profitability momentum, albeit with some uncertainty about sustainability, on a revenue base roughly half the size of PEP's.

---

citation.claim: PepsiCo and Coca-Cola are both highly profitable beverage-and-snack giants, but their profitability profiles differ meaningfully in scale, margin structure, and recent trajectory. PEP is the larger business by revenue—$91.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q2] versus KO's $47.1B [KO:Q2]—yet KO consistently converts a higher share of revenue into profit. KO's gross margin runs well above PEP's: in FY2025, KO generated $29.5B of gross profit on $47.9B of revenue [KO:Q15][KO:Q3], implying ~62% gross margin, while PEP produced $50.9B of gross profit on $93.9B of revenue [PEP:Q15][PEP:Q3], implying ~54% gross margin—a gap that reflects PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and snack manufacturing. At the operating income level, KO's FY2025 result of $13.8B [KO:Q11] on roughly half PEP's revenue base implies an operating margin of ~29%, compared with PEP's $11.5B [PEP:Q11] on nearly double the revenue, implying ~12%. Net income tells a similar story: KO earned $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7] versus PEP's $8.2B [PEP:Q7], a notable reversal given PEP's much larger top line. PEP's FY2025 net income also declined from its FY2024 peak of $9.6B [PEP:Q6], while KO's surged from $10.6B in FY2024 [KO:Q6] to $13.1B in FY2025 [KO:Q7], widening the gap further. Both companies have grown revenues steadily since FY2022, but KO's margin expansion has been more pronounced.

rebuttals.claim: PEP's FY2025 net income declined from its FY2024 peak of $9.6B
rebuttals.reason: The net income decline is supported (C6: $9.578B in FY2024; C7: $8.240B in FY2025), but the original claim understates the breadth of deterioration: PEP operating income also fell sharply from $12.887B (C10) to $11.498B (C11), a ~10.8% drop, indicating the profitability weakness extends well beyond the bottom line and is not solely attributable to below-the-line items.
